# CC-statusline — Bug Report & Fix (Windows + Korean/non-UTF-8 locale)

**Repo:** https://github.com/awesomejun/CC-statusline
**Affected file:** `~/.claude/awesome-statusline.ps1` (generated by `install.ps1`)
**Environment:** Windows 11, Windows PowerShell 5.1, system ANSI code page = `ks_c_5601-1987` (CP949, Korean)

---

## Summary

On Windows machines whose system locale is **not** UTF-8 (e.g. Korean CP949),
the generated `awesome-statusline.ps1` **fails to parse** and the status line
shows nothing at all. The script *is* installed and `settings.json` *is*
configured correctly — but it crashes on every run.

## Symptom

Status line is blank. Running the script manually reveals a parser error:

```
At C:\Users\kang\.claude\awesome-statusline.ps1:161 char:14
+     $Out += "$(Rgb $($End[0]) $($End[1]) $($End[2]))??
+              ~~
Unexpected token '$(' in expression or statement.
Unexpected token '??' in expression or statement.
    + CategoryInfo          : ParserError ... UnexpectedToken
```

Note the `??` — that is where the script's `░` / `█` block-drawing characters
should be. They have been corrupted at *parse* time.

## Root cause

`install.ps1` writes `awesome-statusline.ps1` as **UTF-8 without BOM**.

The script body contains non-ASCII characters (the progress-bar glyphs
`█` U+2588 and `░` U+2591, plus emoji).

**Windows PowerShell 5.1** does not detect UTF-8 automatically. When a `.ps1`
file has **no BOM**, PS 5.1 reads it using the **system ANSI code page**.
On a Korean system that is CP949 — so the UTF-8 byte sequences
(`█` = `E2 96 88`, `░` = `E2 96 91`) are decoded as the wrong characters,
producing garbage tokens (`??`) that break the PowerShell parser.

This does not reproduce on:
- macOS / Linux (the `.sh` variant), or
- Windows machines whose ANSI code page is already UTF-8 (65001), or
- PowerShell 7+ (which defaults to UTF-8).

It reproduces specifically on **PS 5.1 + non-UTF-8 ANSI code page**, which is
the default for many Korean/Japanese/Chinese Windows installs.

## Verification

```powershell
$bytes = [System.IO.File]::ReadAllBytes("$HOME\.claude\awesome-statusline.ps1")
"{0:X2} {1:X2} {2:X2}" -f $bytes[0],$bytes[1],$bytes[2]
# Buggy output: 23 20 41   (= "# A", i.e. NO BOM)
# Fixed output: EF BB BF   (= UTF-8 BOM)

[System.Text.Encoding]::Default.WebName   # ks_c_5601-1987 on affected systems
$PSVersionTable.PSVersion                 # 5.1.x
```

---

## Fix (repo side — recommended)

In `install.ps1`, write the generated `.ps1` file as **UTF-8 *with* BOM** so
Windows PowerShell 5.1 parses it correctly regardless of system code page.

**Do not** rely on `Out-File -Encoding utf8` semantics being consistent — in
PS 5.1 it writes a BOM, but in PS 7+ it writes *no* BOM. Write the BOM
explicitly so the output is identical on every host:

```powershell
$utf8Bom = New-Object System.Text.UTF8Encoding($true)   # $true = emit BOM
[System.IO.File]::WriteAllText($scriptPath, $scriptContent, $utf8Bom)
```

Alternative (more robust, no encoding dependency at all): replace the Unicode
block glyphs with ASCII (e.g. `#` / `-` or `=` / `.`) so the script is pure
ASCII and parses under any code page. The BOM fix is preferred since it keeps
the nicer-looking bars.

> Apply the same fix to any other generated files containing non-ASCII content.

## Fix (user side — until the repo is patched)

Re-save the existing file as UTF-8 with BOM:

```powershell
$path = "$HOME\.claude\awesome-statusline.ps1"
Copy-Item $path "$path.nobom-backup" -Force
$text = [System.IO.File]::ReadAllText($path, [System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8)
$utf8Bom = New-Object System.Text.UTF8Encoding($true)
[System.IO.File]::WriteAllText($path, $text, $utf8Bom)
```

Then restart Claude Code (the status line is loaded at startup).

**Caveat:** re-running the one-line installer (`irm ... | iex`) will overwrite
the file as no-BOM again and reintroduce the bug until the repo is patched.

## Test after fix

```powershell
$json = '{"model":{"display_name":"Opus 4.8"},"workspace":{"current_dir":"C:/Users/kang"},"cwd":"C:/Users/kang","context":{"used_tokens":42000,"max_tokens":200000}}'
$json | powershell -NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File "$HOME\.claude\awesome-statusline.ps1" -Size large
$LASTEXITCODE   # expect 0, with colored bars rendered
```

---

## Enhancement suggestion — show "no git" instead of a blank field

### Problem

When the current directory is **not** a git repository, the git status field
renders as an empty string. In the multi-field layouts (medium/large) this
leaves an awkward empty gap between separators:

```
🤖 Opus 4.8 |  | no env | ...
            ^^ empty git field
```

### Fix

When git is not available, fall back to a muted `no git` label — consistent
with how the environment field already shows `no env`.

In `awesome-statusline.ps1`, right after the git-detection block closes
(`$GitAvailable` / `$GitStatus` setup), add:

```powershell
if (-not $GitAvailable) { $GitStatus = "$($C.Overlay)no git$Reset" }
```

This uses the same muted `Overlay` color as the existing `no env` label, so it
reads as a placeholder rather than real status. Result:

```
🤖 Opus 4.8 | no git | no env | ...
```

The existing branch/clean/dirty logic is guarded by `$GitAvailable`, so real
repos are unaffected — `no git` only appears outside a repository.

---

## Enhancement suggestion — show the full directory path (no `~` shortening)

### Problem

The directory field is passed through a `Short-Path` helper that replaces the
user's home directory with `~`:

```
📂 ~/projects/foo
```

Some users prefer to see the **absolute path** (clearer about exactly where
they are, easier to copy):

```
📂 C:\Users\kang\projects\foo
```

### Fix

Use the raw current directory instead of the shortened form. In
`awesome-statusline.ps1`, change the `$DirDisplay` assignment:

```powershell
# before
$DirDisplay = "📂 $($C.Subtext)$(Short-Path $CurrentDir)$Reset"

# after
$DirDisplay = "📂 $($C.Subtext)$CurrentDir$Reset"
```

`Short-Path` is referenced only at this one site, so it can be removed too if
desired. (Optional: make this configurable via a parameter/env var if you want
to keep `~` shortening as the default for other users.)

---

## Enhancement suggestion — prompt for a size preset during install

### Idea

When no size is passed to the installer, **ask** the user which preset to use
(`xsmall` / `small` / `medium` / `large` / `xlarge`), with a note that
rendered examples are in the GitHub README, and **default to `large`**.

This keeps the existing non-interactive behavior (passing a size argument still
works and skips the prompt), and falls back to `large` when running in a
non-interactive context (e.g. `irm ... | iex` in a script/CI where no console
is attached).

### Example (`install.ps1`)

```powershell
# $Size may already be set from an argument; only prompt if it's empty.
if (-not $Size) {
    if ([Environment]::UserInteractive -and -not [Console]::IsInputRedirected) {
        Write-Host ""
        Write-Host "Choose a status line size preset:"
        Write-Host "  xsmall | small | medium | large | xlarge"
        Write-Host "  (See rendered examples in the README: https://github.com/AwesomeJun/CC-statusline)"
        $answer = Read-Host "Size [default: large]"
        $Size = if ([string]::IsNullOrWhiteSpace($answer)) { 'large' } else { $answer.Trim().ToLowerInvariant() }
    } else {
        $Size = 'large'   # non-interactive (e.g. irm ... | iex) -> default
    }
}

# Validate; fall back to large on anything unrecognized.
$valid = 'xsmall','small','medium','large','xlarge'
if ($valid -notcontains $Size) {
    Write-Host "Unknown size '$Size', using 'large'."
    $Size = 'large'
}
```

`$Size` then flows into the generated `statusLine.command` exactly as today
(`... awesome-statusline.ps1 -Size $Size`). The bash installer (`install.sh`)
should get the equivalent `read -p` prompt for parity.

> Note: with `irm ... | iex`, `Read-Host` only works when an interactive
> console is attached; the `[Console]::IsInputRedirected` guard above ensures a
> clean fallback to `large` otherwise, so piped installs never hang.
